- Kernel version: 6.16.4

following_discussion.discussion_body:
Proton log:
[steam-3919760.tar.gz](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22135487/steam-3919760.tar.gz)

Running the game with `DXVK_CONFIG="dxgi.enableDummyCompositionSwapchain=True"` allows it to run. The performance is okay, but drops all the way to 10-20 fps on zoom in/out and then restores. There is a lot of shadow shimmering on all shadow settings, but low.

@agurenko if you switch to proton experimental bleeding edge https://github.com/ValveSoftware/Proton/wiki/Proton-Versions can you start the game without extra launch parameters? and do the shadow issues you mention remain?
@simifor there is no difference between "stable" (10.0-2) and experimental on both points.
@agurenko not experimental, specifically experimental *bleeding edge*. The link in my previous message shows how to select it

@simifor my bad, so, the bleeding edge does help with a start without extra parameters, but the shadow (I'm not sure it's actually shadow, it might be grass textures themselves) still shimmering (Link to screen recording: https://e.pcloud.link/publink/show?code=XZcTeOZAw3TPTv8o8XsQyVymTmy7S9BHDek)

Adding a bit more information. My issue is definitely proton related. On the same hardware but with windows the Ubisoft connect launcher works fine. However the issue is also not unique to just Anno 117 but shows up for all games using the Ubisoft launcher. So should this be moved into a separate issue then?

(And before anyone says it, also yes, the Intel A380 was not powerful enough for Anno 117 and resulted in about 15 FPS even on Windows)

Looks like obfuscated code but no reason to believe the obfuscator itself is causing problems yet.
This is actually using .NET Core, so wine-mono isn't involved. That limits my ability to monitor what it's doing, and I haven't found anything obvious in wine logs.

The game launches with proton-tkg or GE-Proton7-43, however crashes at login/registration, both versions produce different reports from the games crash handler.
I don't know if providing info from non-valve proton versions is ill advised, but i thought its worth mentioning.

[steam-1895130ge.log](https://github.com/ValveSoftware/Proton/files/10427008/steam-1895130ge.log)
[steam-1895130tkg.log](https://github.com/ValveSoftware/Proton/files/10427009/steam-1895130tkg.log)

Trying more proton-tkg versions (based off wine 7.22, 8.0-rc0 and 8.0-rc4), i have little to no technical knowledge but looks to me like the issue causing crashes on start must have been fixed upstream, but its still having issues with RSA encryption(?)

edit: To clear up potential confusion, i am not trying to report a bug with a non-valve proton version, just providing information that the game *launches* under those versions, which are (to my knowledge) built on newer wine builds compared to Valves.
I'll submit an issue to the maintainer of proton-tkg as advised in their repository.

> Personally, I was never able to run the game without the `noeac` flag, and they disabled it with the latest game update. I don't think they ever enabled EAC for Proton, and now, until they do, pretty sure there's no workaround.

I can confirm, it never worked for me on Arch. No matter the kernel.

It "worked" for my girlfriend on Nobara, but we could never join a game together so she had to use the `-noeac` flag as well. It never worked

To follow up on this, it seems @ruineka was right and DKO apparently uses a version of EAC that depends on an older version of glibc.

The [glibc-eac](https://github.com/Frogging-Family/glibc-eac) package allows Arch users to rebuild glibc while excluding commits that break compatibility with older versions of EAC.
The same workaround should work with any distro.

This fixes it for me, I can run the game normally, without the `noeac` flag.

Edit:
DKO seems to be using the EOS version of EAC, which explains why there are no .dll of .so EAC files. EOS EAC appears to be generally broken with glibc 2.36. 
In any case, this issue is probably closely related to #6051.

following_discussion.discussion_body:
Hello @iodream, please keep in mind that Proton generally requires a Vulkan capable GPU for support. The `PROTON_USE_WINED3D` runtime option is not considered supported and your Sandybridge generation hardware is able to run some games with Proton more by luck than intent. Any issues with games that usually use DXVK or VKD3D-Proton with Proton need to have your issue reproduced with vanilla wine and reported to upstream wine.

Having written that, it looks like there's an issue running this game regardless of hardware.

Thank you for clarifying that. Yes i am aware that my hardware is technically unsupported but i tried to limit my reports to issues which have less to do with performance and rendering and more with general lockups and crashes, which seem to happen to other people as well.

Does this mean i should refrain from future reports?

following_discussion.discussion_body: Hello @ChrisClems, `wine: Unhandled illegal instruction at address 00006FFFFB0962FC (thread 018c), starting debugger...` hints that the game is trying to use a CPU feature that your Sandy Bridge generation does not have, but it's not immediately clear what specific feature that is.

> Hello [@ChrisClems](https://github.com/ChrisClems), `wine: Unhandled illegal instruction at address 00006FFFFB0962FC (thread 018c), starting debugger...` hints that the game is trying to use a CPU feature that your Sandy Bridge generation does not have, but it's not immediately clear what specific feature that is.

Interesting. I don't think I ever would have thought of that. Can you recommend any ways in which I might be able to investigate further?
It looks like this was an unintended AVX2 dependency which has since been patched out. Game is running fine via Proton9+ currently.
